content.text: TypeScript has three special types that don't correspond to a single JavaScript value: any (unsafe wildcard), unknown (safe top type), and never (bottom type). Understanding when to use each is essential for writing both flexible and safe TypeScript code.

attrs.alt: Comparison of any (unsafe escape hatch), unknown (safe top type), and never (bottom type / impossible) in TypeScript

content.text: When a variable is typed as any, TypeScript turns off all type checks for it. You can assign anything to it, access any property on it, and assign it to any other type — without an error.
incremental migration of large JavaScript codebases, rapid prototyping, third-party library types that haven't been updated. Always replace any with a proper type or unknown once the shape is known.
TypeScript can infer any in places where it can't determine a type. The noImplicitAny compiler flag (enabled by strict mode) turns these inferences into errors.
unknown — The Safe Alternative to any
unknown is the type-safe counterpart of any. Like any, you can assign any value to unknown. Unlike any, you cannot operate on an unknown value without first narrowing its type.
unknown is the correct type for values whose shape you don't control at compile time: HTTP responses, JSON.parse results, error objects, and localStorage reads.
Shape not known; narrow before use
Replace as soon as shape is known
only if type is truly unknowable

never is the type with no values. A function that never returns (throws unconditionally or loops forever) has return type never. It is also the type TypeScript infers for a union after all members have been excluded.

never's most practical use is exhaustive union checks. When a union has been narrowed down completely, the remaining branch has type never. assertNever() turns a missing case into a compile-time error.
Can caller assign the value as-is to a typed variable?
TypeScript errors on unguarded property access?
e is unknown in TS 4.4+; .message is an error
Silences all type checks on data
Returning never from a conditional branch
Control flow may look broken to TypeScript
never frequently appears as the output of a conditional type when no case matches. This is the mechanism behind many built-in utility types.
Assigning a value to never at the type level (const _x: never = x) is a compile-time assertion that the value is unreachable. It costs nothing at runtime because the line is dead code.
A type guard is any expression that narrows unknown (or a broad type) to something specific. The three primary forms are: typeof checks, instanceof checks, and user-defined type predicates.
Libraries like Zod parse unknown values at runtime and produce strongly-typed results, replacing manual type guards for complex structures.
Interaction Between any, unknown, and never
Understanding how the three types relate to TypeScript's type hierarchy explains why each behaves the way it does.
unknown is the top type — accepts all values
never is assignable to every type (bottom type)
Nothing can be assigned to never
unknown is a supertype of any
Think of the type system as a set of allowed values.
is the set of all values (assign anything in).
is the empty set (nothing can be assigned in; nothing can go wrong assigning out).
is a hole that bypasses the set logic entirely.
any, unknown, and never serve distinct purposes in TypeScript's type system:
: turns off the type checker — use only as a last resort during JS migration or for genuinely untyped third-party code
: accepts every value (like any) but requires explicit narrowing before use — the correct type for external data, JSON.parse, and catch clauses
: has no values — used for functions that unconditionally throw/loop, and as the bottom of exhaustive union checks
whenever you don't control the input shape; narrow with
in switch/if default branches to get compile-time exhaustiveness guarantees when a union grows
Angular: type HTTP response bodies as
to enforce route and status exhaustiveness
